observations.recommendation: 13. Recognize the depreciation expense for current year procurements of PPEs. 15. Recognize depreciation for PPEs transferred from Trust Fund. 16. Revise the recognized Residual Value for Other Land Improvement account and Other Property, Plant and Equipment account and make the necessary adjustment to correct the understatement of Accumulated Depreciation recognized for these accounts. 17. observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant admitted that not all the depreciable assets were provided with depreciation and assured the Audit Team that for CY 2023 they will implement the recommendations: compute and recognize depreciation, prepare PPELCs, and provide appropriate schedules and disclosures.
observations.reason: Some actions were planned for CY 2023

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1.18.1. direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1.18.1.1. reconcile the accounting records with the property unit and
determine the causes of the variance for proper accounting
thereof;
1.18.1.2. compute and recognize depreciation on all of the depreciable
assets of the Municipality;
1.18.1.3. prepare and maintain PPELCs with complete significant
information; and
1.18.1.4. coordinate with the GSO-Designate and undergo disposition
procedures for non-existing/missing PPE.
1.18.2. create an Inventory Committee to complete the physical count of all
properties as at year-end and henceforth, conduct an annual physical
count of PPE; and
1.18.3. require the Inventory Committee to categorize the identified PPE in the
RPCPPE based on its proper account classification and reconcile the
RPCPPE with the accounting records.

1.19. The concerned personnel admitted that there were lapses in their property and
equipment management. They said that issuance and control of PPEs were not
supported with Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). Nevertheless, they
assured the Audit Team that for CY 2023, they will strive to implement the
recommendations stated in this Audit Observation.

We recommended to the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the funds received
from NGAs to the Trust Fund account of the Municipality.

2.15. Subsequently, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to provide the
journal entries for the transfer of funds received from NGAs to the Trust Fund
account of the Municipality.

2.16. We also recommended to the Municipal Accountant to initiate the return of
the unused balances of funds received from NGAs. For the Congressional
Development Funds (CDF) totaling P135,371.57, we reiterate that the unused
balances of the said funds be returned to the Bureau of the Treasury (BTr).

2.17. Lastly, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to trace the
composition of the unsubstantiated balances to determine if claims against the
Municipality exist. If no claims exist, initiate the request for write-off of the
unsubstantiated balances of the Due to NGAs account.

3.13. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
3.13.1. prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries to:
3.13.1.1. recognize the completed projects to the Buildings and
Road Networks accounts amounting to P1,709,051.54
and P399,199.68, respectively; and
3.13.1.2. properly recognize the Construction in Progress-
Infrastructure Asset account for the accumulated costs
incurred in the construction of road network;
3.13.2. prepare and maintain the CIPLC with complete significant
information on a per project basis;
3.13.3. provide adequate disclosures such as the amount and the status of
the projects under the Construction in Progress account in the
Notes to Financial Statements; and
3.13.4. henceforth, ensure that completed projects are adjusted to its
appropriate asset accounts.

3.14. During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant was amendable with the
recommendations. The Municipal Accountant assured the Audit Team that he will
provide the appropriate accounting entries, will prepare and maintain the CIPLC
and provide proper disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

4.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to:
4.10.1. ensure that all claims for government funds are supported with
complete documentation for processing of payment to various
creditors; and
4.10.2. prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the payment for
Accounts Payable that was erroneously charged to the Other
Maintenance and Operating Expenses account.

4.11. The Municipal Accountant during the exit conference said that he always makes
sure that the recorded accounts payable are all supported with the required
documents. With this Audit Observation, he assured the Audit Team that he will
double his efforts in checking the supporting documents before recording the
appropriate accounts payable account. For the erroneous entry, the Municipal
Accountant will provide the correcting entries

5.16. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
5.16.1. prepare the annual Schedule of the Delinquent Stallholders and
Lessees as at year-end and furnish the same to the Municipal
Accountant; and
5.16.2. prepare Demand Letters indicating the total unpaid monthly
rentals and the penalties thereof to all delinquent stallholders.

5.17. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of
accounts the receivables on the rentals from the Market Stall and the
Government Lots, based on the report forwarded by the Municipal Treasurer
and the corresponding income earned therefrom.

6.16. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
6.16.1. Municipal Accountant to:
6.16.1.1. prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to revert
the stale checks to the Cash in Bank-Local Currency,
Current Account and to recognize the appropriate
payable/liability or equity accounts;
6.16.1.2. trace the difference of P512,637.99 between the reported
balance per books and adjusted balance per BRS and
prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries to correct
the balance of the account; and
6.16.1.3. make representations with LBP San Juan Branch
pertaining to account number 4182-1020-89 that is non-
existent in the books but present per records of the bank.
6.16.2. Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees one
month before the checks become stale. Stale checks shall be
reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the period of
cancellation.

6.17. During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant said that he will record the
adjusting journal entries for the stale checks. Also, the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant assured the Audit Team that they will reconcile their records
starting in CY 2023 to avoid discrepancy between the balance per book, BRS and
Bank Statement. The Municipal Treasurer confirmed that the non-existent account
in the books were actually existing per verification in her records. The Municipal
Accountant admitted that he was not able to record the said account in the books of
account. The unrecorded bank account pertains to the KALAHI account
representing the balance for the initial deposit. Lastly, the Municipal Treasurer
assured the Audit Team that she will send written notices to the payees one month
before the checks become stale.

7.14. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
7.14.1. ascertain the correct balance of the receivable accounts by tracing
the nature of the transaction and its pertinent supporting
documents and prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries to
arrive at the accurate balance of the account;
7.14.2. serve demand letters to the NGAs, LGUs, or individuals concerned
after determination of the valid recipients/debtors, for immediate
liquidation of fund transfers, particularly the long outstanding
accounts;
7.14.3. analyze the accounts that are dormant and long outstanding,
determine if the settlement/collectability could no longer be
ascertained and take the necessary actions for their proper
disposition as provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated
December 19, 2016; and
7.14.4. henceforth, conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and
validation of the existence of the receivables.

7.15. The Municipal Accountant during the exit conference said that they will initiate the
write-off the dormant receivables because some balances in the receivables account
can no longer be collected because the persons indicated in the receivable are
already deceased. They will also serve confirmation letters to NGAs and LGUs or
individuals for long outstanding accounts.

8.16. We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC to submit written
justification why award of contract was made despite having poor performance in
its on-going contracts as disclosed in the Statement of All On-going Government
and Private Contracts Including Contracts Awarded But Not Yet Started.

8.17. We also recommended to the BAC to submit written justification why a
recommendation for an award of contract was made despite the
misrepresentation of data in bidding documents and the omission of data in the
Statement of On-going Contracts Including Contracts Awarded but not yet
Started.

8.18. Lastly, in future procurements of infrastructure projects, we recommended to the
BAC to thoroughly scrutinize the post-qualification of the bidding documents and
to require, where necessary, documents to comprehensively undertake the post-
qualification of bidders.

9.13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to
submit a written justification why the two Contract Time Extensions were granted to
the contractor despite the deficiencies noted above.

9.14. For future transactions, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer evaluate
carefully the request for contract time extensions to avoid the approval of requests
which does not warrant Contract Time Extension.

9.15. Lastly, we recommended that Municipal Engineer evaluate the performance of the
contractor with regard to any negative slippage that may be incurred and take
immediate action thereof, pursuant to pertinent provisions of GPPB Circular 03-2019
dated March 8, 2019 on the Guidelines on Contract Termination Due to Fifteen
Percent (15%) Negative Slippage by the Contractor in Infrastructure Projects.

10.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MPDC and the
Municipal Engineer to resolve the issues on the non-implementation of PPAs
and fast track its implementation.

10.11. Henceforth, the PPAs that shall be approved and appropriated are well
planned and implementation ready.

11.17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
11.17.1. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to:
11.17.1.1. prepare a Revised/Supplemental Local Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan
(LDRRMFIP) incorporating therein the PPAs for
disaster risk reduction and management program that
were not embodied in the original plan; and
11.17.1.2. ensure implementation of approved PPAs incorporated
in the LDRRMFIP and Annual Investment Plan (AIP)
by identifying programs and projects that are feasible
and will improve the LGUs capability to respond to
occurrence of disasters;
11.17.2. Municipal Planning and Development Officer to prepare a
Revised/Supplemental AIP for additional disaster-related projects based on the
approved LDRRMFIP;
11.17.3. Municipal Accountant to:
11.17.3.1. properly monitor the balance of the LDRRMF and
ensure that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the
LDRRMF for CO shall be made continuing in the
General Fund books until the projects funded therefore
are completed and any savings for use in the disaster risk
reduction and management activities as provided in the
LDRRMFIP, in consonance with COA Circular 2012-
002;
11.17.3.2. process only those payments with complete
documentation; and
11.17.3.3. submit immediately the lacking disbursement vouchers
for review and validation by the Audit Team

12.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
12.10.1. Property and Supply Unit to:
12.10.1.1. prepare the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM
for each kind of relief goods to document the receipts,
issuances and balances of in-kind donations for proper
monitoring; and
12.10.1.2. prepare the Inventory List of Donated Items for DRRM
based on the updated Stock Cards and Property Cards;
12.10.2. MSWDO to prepare Acknowledgment Receipts for donated items
received containing complete significant information such as the
date, control numbers, quantity, unit, article, description and value
as required in COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014; and
12.10.3. Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosures pertaining to
DRRM not evident on the face of the financial statements such as
but not limited to the quantity of received, issued and balance of
donated relief goods per category such as food items, medicines and
medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014;

13.17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
13.17.1. Nurse/designated personnel by the MHO to:
13.17.1.1. prepare and maintain an accurate report on the
Summary/List of vaccines received, distributed and
balances based on the updated stock cards;
13.17.1.2. ensure proper monitoring on the movement of COVID-
19 vaccines thru reconciliation with Provincial Health
Services Office (PHSO) as to the correct number of
vaccines received by the RHU based on complete
supporting documents; and
13.17.1.3. coordinate with the PHSO as to the scheduling of the
retrieval of expired vaccines from the RHU.
13.17.2. MHO Head to properly plan the utilization and distribution of COVID-19
vaccines, including nearly expiring vaccines, to minimize or prevent wastage
thereof.

14.12. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
14.12.1. Require the HRMO to assess the existing Organizational Structure
so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be established
and submit a report with recommendations thereon to the Local
Chief Executive;
14.12.2. Create clear-cut policies or written guidelines in the hiring JO
personnel;
14.12.3. Require Job Order personnel to submit primary personal
identification, undergo interview and the HRMO to evaluate the
performance of each JO;
14.12.4. Incorporate in the contract the duration of the work to be
undertaken by the JO personnel, specify a lump sum, piece of work
or intermittent job to be performed and require them to submit
accomplishments reports to support payments of wages; and
14.12.5. Comply with the payment of wages to JOs based on the current daily
minimum wage rate as mandated by the Regional Tripartite
Wages and Productivity Board Wage Order No. RB VIII-22.

15.13. We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to:
15.13.1. Classify the loans payable balance as to its current and non-current
portion in the CY 2023;
15.13.2. Determine the cause of the difference in the balance of loans
payable between the Schedule of Amortization and the Statement
of Financial Position for the MDFO Loan and provide the
correcting entries thereof; and
15.13.3. Confirm with LBP the correct loan amount granted to the LGU per
bank records. Also, make inquiries with the bank, since only 10.5
million was credited to the depository account of the LGU while per
bank confirmation the amount of loan granted was P11million.

that these projects be funded by the LDRRM Fund because according to them, the
projects are disaster related. They have the documents to prove that based on the

2.32. The Audit Team noted that based on the project description that is provided in the
Memorandum of Agreement between the LGU and KALAHI, these projects are
not one of the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management.

3.13. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
3.13.1. Follow strictly the proper recording of Fund Transfers from private
and other government agencies pursuant to the pertinent provisions
of the NGAS Manual for LGUs;
3.13.2. Prepare a JEV to record correcting entries in both Trust Fund and
General Fund to correct the erroneous entries made;
3.13.3. Refund the General Fund for the erroneous charging of expenditure;
and
3.13.4. Initiate the request for write-off of the unsubstantiated balances of the
Due to NGAs account pursuant to COA Circular 2016-006 dated
December 19, 2016.

4.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
4.9.1. Trace the period or date of acquisition of the various PPEs that have
not yet been provided with depreciation and compute the depreciation
based on the estimated useful life of assets as provided under COA
Circular 2003-007 dated December 11, 2003 and DPWH Department
Order No. 176 series of 2015 for Infrastructure Assets. Prior years'
depreciation should be taken up as Prior Years Adjustment to be
closed to Government Equity and current year's depreciation should
be recorded as expense.
4.9.2. Recognize the depreciation expense amounting to P41,829.68 for
current year’s procurements of PPE’s.
4.9.3. Recognized depreciation for PPE transferred from Trust Fund.
4.9.4. Revised the recognized Residual Value for Other Land Improvement
account and Other Property, Plant and Equipment account and make
the necessary adjustment to correct the understatement of
Accumulated Depreciation recognized for these accounts.
4.9.5. Maintain a record or a lapsing schedule detailing the total
depreciation recognized in the current and previous years for each
type of PPE and submit to COA at year-end.

5.18. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record every January of
each year the full amount of RPT due and collectible as receivables based on
the computed amount per RPT Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index
cards.

5.19. We also recommended that the Municipal Assessor:
5.19.1. Conduct physical surveys to verify and determine whether all real
properties within the Municipality are properly listed in the
assessment rolls;
5.19.2. Undertake appraisal and general revision of real property
assessments every three (3) years as required in Section 219 of RA
7160 in order to come up with a reliable amount of assessment on real
property taxes;
5.19.3. Update and reconcile all records pertaining to real properties within
the Municipality and its owners.

5.20. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
5.20.1. Update the Taxpayer’s Index Card and reconcile the same with the
records of the Municipal Assessors;
63
5.20.2. Submit the Taxpayer’s Index Card to the Municipal Accountant at
the start of each year as basis in recording RPT Receivables;
5.20.3. Actively implement tax collection methods and strategies to increase
RPT collections to prevent bloating the balances of uncollected RPT
in the books;
5.20.4. Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which
remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including
reasons for such non-collection, and;
5.20.5. Submit the Certified list of Real Property Tax Delinquencies to the
Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31 of the year
immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were
incurred, and request for assistance from the SB in the enforcement of
the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160.

6.20. We recommended that the BAC:
6.20.1. To make sure that the validity period of the performance bond issued
by the winning bidder for infrastructure projects is sufficient to cover
the contract period and defects liability period of 1 year after
completion of the project.
6.20.2. To carefully scrutinize the bidding documents pursuant to pertinent
provisions of RA 9184
6.20.3. To submit a written justification why they accept performance bonds
from contractors and suppliers with missing and invalid data.

6.21. We also recommended to the Municipal Mayor through the BAC:
6.21.1. To execute contracts within (10) calendars after the receipt of the
Notice of Award.
6.21.2. To issue the Notice to Proceed within (7) calendar days from the
execution of the contract.
6.21.3. To impose the liquidated damages for the delay of the delivery of
goods.
6.21.4. To submit the weather charts for the (2) two projects as a supporting
document for the grant of time extensions.
6.21.5. To submit justification for the discrepancies of the dates of Notice of
Award, Notice to Proceed and Inspection Report for the “Emergency
Procurement of Materials for COVID-19 patients."

7.13. We recommended that the BAC submit justifications why the two (2)
contractors were each awarded with two (2) new contracts despite
misrepresenting themselves by not declaring all their on-going contracts and
not using the prescribed format for the List of Equipment. The justification
is necessary for the proper disposition in post - audit of the payments to the
contractors, if to be disallowed in audit.

7.14. We also recommended that the BAC scrutinize carefully the eligibility
documents submitted by interested bidders so that any misrepresentations
will be detected and to declare as eligible only those who strictly comply with
the provisions of the RIRR of RA 9184.

8.16. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
8.16.1. Must prioritize the re-channeling of funds to other development
projects if the approved PPAs could no longer be implemented due to
causes not attributable to the fault or negligence of the Municipality.
Direct the Municipal Engineer and MPDC to fast-track and monitor
the implementation of the PPAs.
8.16.2. Refrain from executing contract involving the expenditure of public
funds unless there is an appropriation.
8.16.3. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to
prepare and submit quarterly reports on the utilization of the 20%
DF consistent with the reporting requirements prescribed by the
DOF-BLGF. Submit a justification why she allowed the
“Construction of Hinundayan Commercial Town Center
Bldg. II Phase I” despite the lack of appropriation.
8.16.4. The above audit recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of
Notice of Disallowance for the excess of the contract cost of P259,701.68
from the appropriation for the project “Construction of Hinundayan
Commercial Town Center Bldg. II Phase I”.

9.20. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
9.20.1. Rural Health Unit personnel and other concerned offices to submit
lacking supporting documents/ records/ orders to the Audit Team for
payment of SRA and AHDP and accordingly observe compliance on
the documentary requirements for these kinds of personnel incentives
to avoid suspension in audit;
9.20.2. Municipal Accountant to ensure all necessary supporting documents
are attached to claims before affixing his certification in the DV; and
9.20.3. Municipal Accountant to follow the proper recording of transactions
to achieve faithful presentation of accounts in the financial statements,
pursuant to IPSAS I.

10.13. We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:
10.13.1. Require the Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the
payees one month before the checks become stale. Stale checks shall
be reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the period of
cancellation.
10.13.2. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary
adjusting entries for the stale checks over 6 months but not over 2
years amounting to P322,933.44, stale checks that remained
oustanding for more than 2 years amounting to P268,392.75,
unrecorded bank debit memo of P169,055.16 and unrecorded
interest earned of P2,309.24.
• For stale checks over 6 months but not over 2 years:

CR: Accounts Payable P290.20
• For stale checks over 2 years:

CR: Prior Years Adjustment P268,392.75
(*to represent surplus adjustment)
• For unrecorded bank debit memo

CR: Accounts Payable P169,055.16
• For unrecored interest income

CR: Prior Year’s Adjustment P2,309.24
(*to represent surplus adjustment)
10.13.3. Require the Municipal Accountant to trace the unreconciled balance
P541,122.64 between the book and bank balances and make the
necessary adjustments thereof.
10.13.4. Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to
reconcile their records monthly to provide accurate balances in the
cash in bank and in order to make necessary adjustment for
dicrepancies that may be noted, if there are any.

11.23. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require both the BAC
Chairman and Secretariat, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant,
to take the following courses of actions:
11.23.1. Prepare the Project Requirement for emergency procurement under
Bayanihan Act, pursuant to GPPB Circular 01-2020
11.23.2. For each procurement, submit notarized Omnibus Sworn Statement
of suppliers and written recommendations from the BAC, End-user
or any unit authorized by HoPE of the award of contract.
11.23.3. For future transactions, provide in the disbursement vouchers a
recommendation from the BAC or any unit duly authorized by the
Head of Procuring Entity (HoPE) of the award of contract to
suppliers.
11.23.4. Provide a warranty clause in the Purchase Order or Contract
Agreement and properly fill-out all necessary information in the PO
and Contract Agreement like the delivery term and payment term.
11.23.5. For future transactions, post the Notice of Award, Contract or
Purchase Order and other information relative to emergency
procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the website of the PE or if
none, any conspicuous place in the premise of the PE and GPPB
online portal.

11.24. We also recommended to the Municipal Accountant to follow the perpetual
inventory method on the purchase and issuance of inventory pursuant to
Section 114 Volume 1 of MNGAS for LGUs.

12.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all the Revenue
Collection Clerks to remit to the Municipal Treasurer the full amount of
their collections on a daily basis regardless of the nature of collections made.

12.11. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all her
collections, as well as collections turned over to her by the Revenue
Collecting Clerks, daily or not later than the next banking day.

1.8 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require:
A. the Municipal Accountant to:
a. conduct Analysis of PPE accounts to identify, investigate and clear
reconciling items;
b. perform periodic reconciliation of property cards with that of the
Supply Officer/GSO’s records to ensure that all discrepancies are
immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of
accounts;
c. prepare necessary journal entries to adjust the duly investigated
reconciling items in order to reflect the correct account balances;
d. maintain a complete and accurate PPE Ledger Card (PPELC) for
each class of equipment.
B. the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the Report on Physical
Count of Property, Plant and Equipment after reconciling the records
with the Municipal Accountant’s general ledger records so that a reliable
PPE account is presented.

2.9 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant,
General Services Officer and Municipal Engineer to strictly follow the accounting
and reporting procedures mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008 and COA
Circular No. 2016-004 for their guidance in making necessary journal entries to
record back to the PPE account all Public Infrastructures that was transferred to
Registry of Public Infrastructure.

3.10 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare the BRS for the
reconciliation of the cash in bank balances and accounting records and
effect the necessary adjustments, where appropriate;
b. require the Municipal Treasurer to submit the disbursement vouchers
and its supporting documents to the accounting section as basis in the
recording in the books of accounts.

4.8 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to: a) identify and trace the causes of the unreconciled balances of the
reciprocal accounts; and b) maintain and update regularly the subsidiary
ledgers of the said accounts and reconcile with the balances in the general
ledger.

5.6 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Assessor to
expedite the necessary documents in the processing of land titles in favor of
the Municipality. We also recommended that no improvements be made on the
land unless proof of ownership is established through a certificate of title.

6.6 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant:
a. to send within ten (10) days before the expiration of the prescribed
period a written reminder under signature of the Mayor or his duly
authorized representative enjoining officials and employees
concerned to settle their cash advance;
b. not to sign claims for additional cash advance until the previous cash
advance is settled;
c. to cause the immediate withholding of salaries and claims of officers
and employees who failed to settle their cash advances within the
prescribed period after being sent notice and demand;
d. to furnish all department heads listings of all un-liquidated cash
advances of employees under their charge to enhance efforts to
effect liquidation.

7.7 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to see to it that expenses charged against the 20% DF is in accordance with
prescribed guidelines.

7.8 We likewise recommended to the Municipal Mayor, being the head of the
Municipal Development Council to strictly require the preparation of the Annual
Investment Plan which should contain a detailed information of the specific
program, project and activities and which should partake the nature of
investment or capital expenditures.

8.5 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to maintain a special account under the General Fund for 20% DF pursuant to
Section 313 of the R.A. No. 7160.

9.7 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to: 1) require the BAC Chairman to
initiate appropriate timing/phasing of related LDRRMF projects/activities as part
of their procurement planning, specifically the bidding and other related
procurement activities, in order to avoid delays in its implementation; 2) require
the LDRRM Officer and Municipal Engineer to implement promptly the PPAs
included in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to reduce disaster risks and enhance
disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.

10.9 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Accountant to provide sufficient and appropriate disclosure on the Loans
Payable-Domestic in the Notes to Financial Statements for the proper
assessment of the liquidity and solvency of the municipality.

13.9 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to trace the subsidiary accounts with negative balances on the period when an
over-remittance is made and effect the necessary adjustment to correct the
discrepancies, thus, present correct balances of Due to GSIS, Due to
PAG-IBIG and Due to Philhealth in the financial statements.

14.9 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Accountant to adapt monitoring mechanism to detect variances in the
amount to be remitted and withheld. Furthermore, we also recommended
that accounting records such as cash receipts journal, check disbursement
journal, cash disbursement journal, general and subsidiary ledgers must be
updated monthly in order to determine the exact amount to be remitted to
the Bureau of Internal Revenue.

15.7 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to exercise due diligence in the processing of claims and complete
documentations must be observed before effecting payment.

16.8 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer
to promptly turn-over documents and reports under his responsibility on the
prescribed time which shall be duly covered by transmittal letters to the
Municipal Accountant who shall submit not later than the fifth (5th) day of the
ensuing month to the Audit Team for audit.

17.6 We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to adopt a control system in
the preparation of Journal Entry Vouchers as basis for recording all LGU
financial transactions.

18.5 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Accountant to see to it that all disbursement vouchers are numbered in
accordance with the provisions aforecited.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor: a) direct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of PPE and come up with the Report on Physical Count of PPE; b) direct the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation on some properties and to reconcile its records against the records of the General Services Office.

observations.recommendation:

expedite the processing of land titles so as not to put the municipality in a

reclassify the balance of the DRRM Fund of P7,586,141.14 recorded as Due to

Accountant to submit evidence of settlement for the issuance of a Notice of
Settlement of Suspension, Disallowances and Charges and to enforce

Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Likewise, all employees

and Development Officer to train personnel manning VAW Help Desks to
capacitate them to address cases of violence against women in a gender-
responsive manner.

to maintain a special account under the General Fund for 20% DF pursuant to
Section 313 of the R.A. No. 7160.

13.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget
Officer to prepare an Annual Investment Plan containing the detailed
information of the specific program, project and activities in consonance with
the guidelines.

prepare liquidation reports for projects already completed and return the

source agency. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Award Committee to prioritize projects

establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System.

16.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the SWM Focal Person
to consider the appropriate means of communication depending on the
information that it wishes to pass on to the public and its stakeholders. For
instance, there should be public notices on television and on the radio about
who can access the landfill, the working hours at the landfill to limit after-
hours access and the schedule for collection of wastes from households for
the different zones.

16.12 Websites have become increasingly effective and economical in
disseminating information. Therefore it is important to have a specific link
for Solid Waste Management within the Municipality’s website so that it is
easier for the public to have access to information and policy on solid waste
management as well as any issue concerning solid waste management. To
ensure effective implementation of the solid waste management plan, a well-
designed , current and targeted community awareness program is required.
The targeted audience should be considered when determining how the
information should be given to the public. The urgency of the matter should
also be considered so that everyone is aware of the matter as soon as
possible.

17.5 We recommended that a mechanism to measure the volume and type of
waste should be placed at the site, which will provide baseline data to assist
in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for
waste management.

18.7 We recommended that a proper gate at the entrance to the residual
containment area must be built so that it is easier for the personnel at the
RCA to stop vehicles coming to the RCA at the gate, inspect the waste to
confirm the type of waste to be disposed then direct the vehicle to the
designated area depending on the type of waste already inspected. This daily
process will compensate for the non-segregation of waste at source and will
ensure that wastes being disposed at the RCA are properly separated.

18.8 We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the SWM Focal
Person to implement a color coding or appropriate means of identification to
ensure that wastes are properly segregated at the RCA.

19.5 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the SWM Focal Person to
maintain disposal site records in the residual containment area.

20.5 We recommended that the RCA be fenced with a secured gate at the entrance
to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers. This will also help address the
occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

21.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the allocation of funds to
purchase the appropriate safety clothing as prescribed in the Manual for
Operation and Maintenance at Sanitary Landfill; the SWM Focal Person
should ensure that any person entering the RCA must wear the proper safety
clothing so that health risks are minimized and the operations at the RCA will
be safe.

22.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
regularly reconcile the amounts withheld for each employee and remitted to
GSIS in order to ascertain the accuracy and reliability of the balances of Due to
GSIS, Due to PAG-IBIG and Due to Philhealth in the financial statements.

23.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to reconcile her records with the GSIS.

24.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) direct the Municipal
Accountant to closely monitor the withholding of taxes (b) the Municipal
Treasurer to remit promptly and intact the same to the BIR to avoid interest
and penalties on delayed remittances and accumulation of unremitted taxes in
compliance to BIR Regulations.

24.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer only remit the exact
amount deducted from the payroll and payments to suppliers to avoid over
remittance to BIR.

25.5 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to maintain and update the accounting records particularly the general ledgers
and subsidiary ledgers in order to check accuracy of the recording of the
Municipal Treasurer.

26.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
assign personnel who will review the report before it is submitted to audit
team.
